title: Difference Array
description: Prefix Sum's mirror image: O(1) range updates instead of O(1) range queries, reconstructed with a prefix sum at the end.
objectives: Understand the problem difference arrays solve: many range updates
Apply a range update in O(1) using a difference array
Reconstruct the final array using a prefix sum
See the direct relationship between this and Prefix Sum

sections.text: Prefix Sum made many range queries fast on a fixed array. Difference Array solves the mirror-image problem: making many range updates fast, when you only need the final result at the end.
A difference array stores, at each index, the difference between that element and the previous one — letting a range update be applied in O(1) by marking only its two boundaries.
4. Applying a Range Update in O(1)

diff[l] += val marks "starting here, add val." diff[r + 1] -= val marks "starting here, stop adding it." Neither line touches the range's actual elements — each update is just two array writes, O(1), no matter how large the range is.

This is exactly the prefix sum computation from Prefix Sum, applied to the difference array instead of the original one. Taking the prefix sum of a difference array "undoes" the differencing and reveals the final, fully-updated values.

sections.title: Verifying it
points: Indices 1-3: +5 from the first update → indices 1,2,3 get +5
Indices 2-5: +2 from the second update → indices 2,3,4,5 get +2
Index 1: 5. Index 2: 5+2=7. Index 3: 5+2=7. Index 4: 2. Index 5: 2 — matches 0 5 7 7 2 2

sections.title: Marking a calendar's start and end dates instead of filling in every day
sections.text: Booking a week-long vacation doesn't require writing "on vacation" on all seven individual calendar pages one at a time — you could just note "starts here" on day 1 and "ends here" on day 8. Marking many separate trips this way, then reading through the calendar once at the very end to see the final combined schedule, is exactly the difference array technique.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the difference array needs one extra slot beyond the original array's size, for the r + 1 boundary marker.
Applying updates directly to the real array when many range updates are needed — defeats the purpose; mark boundaries in the difference array instead.
Forgetting to reconstruct the final array with a prefix sum pass at the end — the difference array alone isn't the answer, it's an intermediate representation.
Off-by-one errors on the r + 1 boundary — it marks where the effect STOPS, one past the last affected index.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What problem does a difference array solve?
questions.answer: Applying many range updates to an array efficiently, deferring the actual per-element work until a single final reconstruction pass.

questions.question: How do you apply a range update, adding val to indices l through r, using a difference array?
questions.answer: diff[l] += val; diff[r + 1] -= val; — marking where the effect starts and where it stops, without touching the range's individual elements.

questions.question: How do you get the final array from a difference array?
questions.answer: Take its prefix sum — the same running-total computation as Prefix Sum, applied to the difference array.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of applying k range updates and then reconstructing the final array, using a difference array?
questions.answer: O(k) for the updates (O(1) each) plus O(n) for the final reconstruction, versus O(k × range length) for naive direct updates.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A difference array is the mirror image of prefix sum: instead of making range queries fast, it makes range updates fast. Adding a value to every index from l to r becomes just two O(1) writes — diff[l] += val and diff[r + 1] -= val — marking where the effect starts and stops, without touching any of the range's actual elements. After all updates are applied, taking the prefix sum of the difference array reconstructs the final, fully-updated array in O(n). This turns k potentially expensive range updates into k O(1) operations plus one O(n) pass at the end.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Difference Array
sections.text: Difference array = mark 'starts here' and 'stops here' on a calendar, don't fill in every day. Prefix-sum the difference array at the end to reveal the real schedule.
